original_abstract: GROMACS has matured over the last two decades into one of the most widely used packages for biomolecular simulation, evolving from a lean, efficient engine for small systems into a sophisticated heterogeneous framework designed for the world’s largest supercomputers. In this work, we detail the strategies employed to achieve this scalability, focusing on multi-level parallelization and a rigorous commitment to absolute performance. Release 4.6 of GROMACS introduces SIMD acceleration across diverse architectures, GPU offloading, and a hybrid parallelization model using OpenMP and MPI to manage communication both within and between nodes. This push for hardware acceleration necessitated a fundamental re-evaluation of core molecular simulation algorithms, particularly neighbor-searching techniques. We further explore the challenges associated with reaching exascale performance—emphasizing the need for fine-grained task parallelism—and describe the modernization of our software management practices, including peer review and continuous integration, which are essential for maintaining a codebase of this complexity.
humanized_abstract: From modest beginnings GROMACS has evolved over the last two decades into one of the most widely used biomolecular simulation programs in the world. What began as a fast program to simulate small biological systems has developed into a flexible, heterogeneous program capable of simulations on some of the worlds largest supercomputers. This article describes the strategies that have been employed and the tactics that have been employed to achieve high performance, an absolute that is needed at all levels to enable a simulation to reach its full potential. In this paper we describe the new features of GROMACS 4.6 including SIMD support on all modern architectures, GPU acceleration, as well as new hybrid OpenMP/ MPI multi-level parallelism that distributes the workload between different levels of the simulation within a node as well as between different nodes. In order to support the new hardware in the simulations some of the basic neighbor-searching algorithms had to be almost rewritten from scratch. In this article we highlight the challenge of achieving exascale performance by the necessity of having very fine grain parallelism in such simulations and also describe the many software development tools and procedures that have been implemented to develop a simulation code of such complexity.
